title: Isaiah 24
subtitle: The Isaiah Apocalypse Begins: The Earth Devastated

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — “The Earth Is Defiled by Its People”

heb.gloss: to devastate / to empty / to lay waste
heb.paragraph: "See, the LORD is going to lay waste (bālaq) the earth and devastate it" (v.1). THE Isaiah Apocalypse (chs.24–27) — cosmic judgment beyond any single nation. The earth is emptied, scattered, stripped (v.1). Priest and people, master and servant, buyer and seller — all alike (v.2). The earth dries up; the world languishes (v.4). WHY? "The earth is defiled by its people; they have disobeyed the laws, violated the statutes and broken the everlasting covenant" (v.5). A CURSE consumes the earth (v.6). Joy gone: wine dries up, merry-making silenced, songs stopped, beer bitter (vv.7–11). The city of chaos is desolate (v.10). "Only a few are left, as when an olive tree is beaten" (v.13) — the remnant metaphor again.

notes.note: "The LORD is going to lay waste the earth. It will be the same for priest as for people, for master as for servant." No exceptions. No class immunity. Universal devastation.

"The earth is defiled by its people; they have broken the everlasting covenant. Therefore a curse consumes the earth." The "everlasting covenant" — probably the Noahic covenant (Gen 9). Humanity has violated the most basic creation-order. The curse of Genesis 3 reaches its culmination.
"The ruined city lies desolate. Gladness is banished from the earth. Only a few are left." The city of chaos (tohu — the same word as Genesis 1:2). Creation is un-created. But: a remnant remains.

refs.note: Cosmic judgment echoing the Flood: 'the earth is defiled by its people' (v.5) recalls the pre-Flood corruption.
Jeremiah's vision of creation undone uses similar imagery: formless, empty, no light.
The cosmic disturbances at the sixth seal parallel Isaiah's vision of shaken heavens and trembling earth.
The elements dissolving by fire — Peter draws on Isaiah's cosmic judgment tradition.

hist.context: Chapters 24-27 are often called the 'Isaiah Apocalypse' because they shift from oracles against specific nations to cosmic, universal judgment. The scope expands from Babylon, Moab, and Tyre to 'the earth' itself. The 'everlasting covenant' broken (v.5) likely refers to the Noahic covenant (Gen 9:16) — humanity's universal obligations, not just Israel's. This section may have been composed or edited later than the surrounding oracles, but it functions canonically as the climax of the judgment section.

notes.note: Oswalt notes the comprehensive nature: priest and people, master and servant, buyer and seller — all social distinctions dissolve in judgment. No one escapes by status.
The earth 'mourns and withers' because humanity has violated its fundamental obligations. Oswalt sees ecological catastrophe as consequence of moral catastrophe — the land suffers for human sin.

Childs reads chapter 24 as the theological climax of chapters 13-23: the individual nation-oracles now expand to universal scope. What was true of Babylon, Moab, and Tyre is true of all human civilization apart from God.
The 'everlasting covenant' is not Sinai but the primal covenant with all humanity (Gen 9). Even nations without Torah have violated their fundamental obligations to the Creator.

sections.header: Verses 14–23 — “The LORD Will Reign on Mount Zion”

heb.gloss: he reigns / he has become king
heb.paragraph: "The LORD Almighty will reign (mālak̅) on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem, and before its elders — with great glory" (v.23). From the west the remnant raises their voices and shouts for joy (v.14). From the east: glory to the Righteous One (v.16). But Isaiah says: "Woe to me! The treacherous betray!" (v.16). Terror, pit, and snare await the inhabitants (v.17). The floodgates of the heavens open; the foundations of the earth shake (v.18) — the flood-imagery returns. The earth reels like a drunkard, sways like a hut in the wind (v.20). "In that day the LORD will punish the powers in the heavens above and the kings on the earth below" (v.21). Moon ashamed, sun disgraced — because the LORD’s glory outshines them (v.23).

notes.note: "From the ends of the earth we hear singing: ‘Glory to the Righteous One.’ But I said, ‘I waste away! Woe to me!’" The global praise is real AND the prophet’s anguish is real. Both coexist. The treachery hasn’t ended.

"The earth is broken up, the earth is split asunder, the earth is violently shaken. The earth reels like a drunkard." Three verbs of breaking; one simile of drunkenness. The planet itself is destabilised.

"The LORD will punish the powers in the heavens above and the kings on the earth below. The moon will be dismayed, the sun ashamed, when the LORD reigns on Mount Zion." Two-tier judgment: heavenly and earthly. Then God’s reign outshines sun and moon. Rev 21:23 develops this.

refs.note: The heavenly chorus shouting 'the LORD reigns' — Isaiah's remnant praise anticipates Revelation's throne-room worship.
'The LORD reigns, let the earth be glad' — the same cosmic kingship celebrated in the psalms.
The terror of the LORD's appearing — an earlier Isaianic passage with similar imagery of hiding in caves from divine majesty.

hist.context: The 'city of chaos' (v.10) is unnamed — deliberately universal. It represents human civilization in rebellion, whether Babylon, Tyre, or any proud city. The remnant sings from 'the ends of the earth' (v.16), suggesting a worldwide community of the faithful amidst universal judgment. The treachery upon treachery (v.16) may reflect Isaiah's frustration with Judah's political machinations with Egypt and Assyria.

notes.note: Oswalt sees a dramatic shift: amidst judgment, worship breaks out. The remnant on the 'coastlands' and 'ends of the earth' glorify God. Even in catastrophe, faith persists.

The 'host of heaven' punished alongside 'kings of the earth' — Oswalt connects this to the ancient Near Eastern concept of patron deities. Both heavenly and earthly powers fall before Yahweh's universal reign.

Childs emphasizes the tension between the remnant's praise (vv.14-16a) and Isaiah's lament (v.16b). The prophet sees both the ultimate victory and the present treachery. This dialectic of hope and grief characterizes prophetic existence.
The sun and moon 'ashamed' before Yahweh's glory anticipates Revelation 21:23 — no need for created light when the Creator is present. Childs sees this as proto-eschatological theophany.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVit will be the same for priest as for people, for the master as for his servant, for the mistress as for her servant, for seller as for buyer, for borrower as for lender, for debtor as for creditor.ESVAnd it shall be, as with the people, so with the priest; as with the slave, so with his master; as with the maid, so with her mistress; as with the buyer, so with the seller; as with the lender, so with the borrower; as with the creditor, so with the debtor.NIVTherefore a curse consumes the earth; its people must bear their guilt. Therefore earth’s inhabitants are burned up, and very few are left.ESVTherefore a curse devours the earth, and its inhabitants suffer for their guilt; therefore the inhabitants of the earth are scorched, and few men are left.

rec.text: ""The LORD is going to lay waste the earth. It will be the same for priest as for people, for master as for servant." No exceptions. No class immunity. Universal devastation."

rows.label: The LORD Reigns — 24:14–23
rows.text: Earth reels like a drunkard. "The LORD will REIGN on Mount Zion — with great GLORY." Moon ashamed; sun disgraced.

hebtext.gloss: to devastate / to empty / to lay waste
hebtext.note: "See, the LORD is going to lay waste (bālaq) the earth and devastate it" (v.1). THE Isaiah Apocalypse (chs.24–27) — cosmic judgment beyond any single nation. The earth is emptied, scattered, stripped (v.1). Priest and people, master and servant, buyer and seller — all alike (v.2). The earth dries up; the world languishes (v.4). WHY? "The earth is defiled by its people; they have disobeyed the laws, violated the statutes and broken the everlasting covenant" (v.5). A CURSE consumes the earth (v.6). Joy gone: wine dries up, merry-making silenced, songs stopped, beer bitter (vv.7–11). The city of chaos is desolate (v.10). hebtext.gloss: he reigns / he has become king
hebtext.note: "The LORD Almighty will reign (mālak̅) on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem, and before its elders — with great glory" (v.23). From the west the remnant raises their voices and shouts for joy (v.14). From the east: glory to the Righteous One (v.16). But Isaiah says: "Woe to me! The treacherous betray!" (v.16). Terror, pit, and snare await the inhabitants (v.17). The floodgates of the heavens open; the foundations of the earth shake (v.18) — the flood-imagery returns. Moon ashamed, sun disgraced — because the LORD’s glory outshines them (v.23).

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10: cosmic devastation, everlasting covenant broken, earth un-created. Sovereignty at 9: God reigns, outshining sun and moon. The Isaiah Apocalypse begins.

coaching.tip: Chapter 24 marks a dramatic shift. After ten chapters of oracles against specific nations, Isaiah zooms out to cosmic judgment — 'the LORD is going to lay waste the earth' (v. 1). The earth itself is 'defiled by its people' who have 'broken the everlasting covenant' (v. 5). This isn't one nation's judgment but creation's. Scholars call chapters 24–27 'Isaiah's Apocalypse' — the Bible's first sustained apocalyptic vision.

'The LORD will reign on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem, and before its elders — with great glory' (v. 23). The sun and moon are 'ashamed' before God's radiance. This verse feeds directly into Revelation 21:23, where the New Jerusalem needs no sun because God's glory illuminates it. Isaiah's cosmic vision provides the architectural blueprint for John's apocalypse.
